feedingProfile.preferredNpk: high-potassium (e.g. 5-5-10) for containers; general-purpose granular in early spring for in-ground

commonIssues: Apple is a deciduous tree (standard trees reach 4.5-9 m / 15-30 ft); in-ground planting is strongly preferred. For containers use only a dwarfing rootstock (e.g. M27, M9, M26); the recommendedMinLiters (38 L) is a realistic dwarf-apple container minimum, not a size for standard or semi-dwarf trees.

Most apple varieties are self-unfruitful and set only a small crop with their own pollen; plant at least one compatible second variety (or a flowering crabapple) with an overlapping bloom period within ~15-30 m (50-100 ft) for a good crop.
Late spring frosts damage open flowers and reduce fruit set; choose a sheltered, non-frost-pocket site and consider late-flowering varieties in cold areas.
Container trees dry out fast and may need watering daily in hot weather; keep compost evenly moist but never waterlogged (root rot risk).
Most classic apple varieties are high-chill (need roughly 800+ hours below ~6 C / 45 F); low-chill needs vary by cultivar and climate.

source.citation: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Malus domestica (Apple): full sun (6+ hours direct sun) to partial shade; USDA hardiness zones 4a-9b; slightly acidic, moist, well-drained soil (pH 6.0-6.5); deciduous tree 15-30 ft mature height; grafted dwarf rootstocks much smaller.

North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Malus domestica: USDA hardiness zones 4a-9b. Zone 4a average annual minimum temperature is about -34 C (-30 F), used as coldHardyMinC for cold-hardy cultivars.

Iowa State University Extension and Outreach — Pollination requirements for tree and small fruits: apples are self-unfruitful (most varieties set only a small crop with their own pollen); plant at least two different varieties with overlapping bloom for cross-pollination; flowering crabapples can pollinate nearby apples; space cross-pollinating trees within 50-100 ft.

RHS — How to grow apples (grow your own): full sun, warm sheltered site not prone to late frosts which damage flowers; container apples on M9/M26 dwarf rootstock need a pot at least 45 cm (18 in) diameter with loam-based peat-free compost; keep compost evenly moist, water regularly in dry spells; feed containers with high-potassium liquid fertiliser every fortnight in the growing season; in-ground trees in regularly mulched soil generally need no extra feeding.

RHS — How to grow apples: container apples on dwarf rootstock need a pot of at least 45 cm (18 in) diameter; a ~45 cm cube/round pot is roughly 38-45 L, used for recommendedMin/idealMin container volume. Frost damages open flowers (frostSensitive).

RHS — How to grow apples: keep container compost evenly moist and water regularly in dry spells; apples do not tolerate prolonged drought but dislike waterlogging. baseIntervalDays (3) and hotWeatherMultiplier reflect 'evenly moist' for an established in-ground/large-container tree; container trees may need more frequent watering (daily in heat).

Confidence note: schema numeric watering interval (baseIntervalDays=3) and hotWeatherMultiplier are conservative estimates. RHS and NCSU specify 'evenly moist / regular watering' qualitatively rather than a day interval, and actual frequency depends heavily on rootstock, in-ground vs container, soil and climate (container trees can need daily watering in heat). Treated as an estimate.
